one_line_takeaway: A small GRPO-trained ReAct agent can autonomously craft tiny, stealthy instruction edits that reliably degrade frozen black-box robot VLA policies (−20.6% task success, +55% action length, +33% constraint violations on LIBERO) more efficiently than a GPT-5-mini attacker.
key_finding: On LIBERO across six state-of-the-art VLAs, SABER on average reduces task execution rate by 20.6% absolute (overall Base TER 93.9% → Attack TER 73.3%, Table I), increases action-sequence length by ~55% (overall AIR = 1.55, Table II), and increases constraint violations by ~33% (overall CVI = 1.33, Table III). Effects vary by suite: planning-heavy LIBERO-Goal is most vulnerable (ASR 48.9%, CVI 2.59), while grounding-heavy Spatial/Object are less amplified. Versus a frozen GPT-5-mini attacker on the identical tool interface (Table V), SABER attains slightly better objective metrics (ASR 16.7 vs 14.5, AIR 1.38 vs 1.37, CVI 1.27 vs 1.25, ~2% absolute average gain) while being far stealthier: 21.1% fewer tool calls (3.10 vs 3.93) and 54.7% fewer character edits (76.46 vs 168.8). The cold-start ablation (Table VI) shows GRPO-only training collapses to ineffective low-cost attacks (ASR 8.7% vs 16.7% for SFT+GRPO). Token-level edits dominate after training, and Task Failure uses only ~10.6% of the 200-character edit budget (Table IV).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is SABER, an agent-centric black-box attack framework that automatically generates minimal, plausible instruction-level adversarial edits against frozen vision-language-action (VLA) robot policies. Its novel elements are: (1) formulating instruction-only black-box VLA attacks as a constrained sequential-decision/optimization problem over robot behavioral objectives under a bounded edit budget; (2) a single GRPO-trained ReAct-style attacker that adaptively composes character-, token-, and prompt-level perturbations via a two-stage FIND→APPLY tool protocol, without gradient access to the target VLA; and (3) targeting three behavior-level objectives beyond binary task failure — task failure, action inflation (unnecessarily long execution), and constraint violation. This is distinct from cited prior work (e.g., RoboPAIR, Wu et al., FreezeVLA, AttackVLA, VLA-Fool, BadVLA, and LLM red-teaming methods like PAIR/AutoDAN/AdvPrompter), which the authors characterize as relying on manual/heuristic/GPT-search perturbations or specific attack classes rather than a learned, reusable, budget-aware attacker.
threat_model: Black-box, instruction-only adversary against a frozen VLA robot policy. The attacker has no access to model parameters or gradients (through either the policy or its downstream execution) and can only observe rollouts of the target VLA in simulation. It perturbs only the textual natural-language task instruction (not perception/vision), and operates under realistic stealth/budget constraints motivated by short, easily-inspected robot instructions: at most 200 character-level edits (Levenshtein) and at most 4 FIND→APPLY tool-chain calls per episode. The adversarial goal is targeted behavioral degradation of physical robot execution — task failure, action inflation, or constraint/safety violation — rather than merely altering text output. The paper frames this as a red-teaming / robustness-evaluation tool for stress-testing VLA systems before deployment.
limitations: The authors explicitly state the study is limited to text-only instruction perturbations in simulation (LIBERO), leaving multimodal (joint language+perception) and real-world physical attack surfaces unexplored. Attacks target only the instruction channel, not the victim's intermediate reasoning/plans or tool-selection logic (named as future work). Additional reviewer-noted limits grounded in the text: the improvement over the GPT-5-mini baseline on objective effectiveness is small (~2% absolute), so the headline advantage is primarily efficiency/stealth rather than raw potency; training used a single victim (π0.5) with transfer evaluated on others; test evaluation is small-scale (3 held-out tasks/suite, 5 episodes/task); and some cells show attacks occasionally making rollouts safer/more efficient (e.g., negative-direction CV entries), indicating inconsistent per-model effects.

datasets_used: LIBERO benchmark (LIBERO-Spatial, LIBERO-Object, LIBERO-Goal, LIBERO-Long suites)
quantitative_results: Overall task execution rate: 93.9% (baseline) → 73.3% (attacked); average ASR = 20.6% (Table I)
Average action inflation ratio AIR = 1.55 overall (~55% longer action sequences); highest on LIBERO-Goal AIR = 2.56 (Table II)
Average constraint violation inflation CVI = 1.33 overall (~33% more violations); LIBERO-Goal CVI = 2.59 (Table III)
vs GPT-5-mini attacker (Table V): ASR 16.7 vs 14.5, AIR 1.38 vs 1.37, CVI 1.27 vs 1.25; Tool Calls 3.10 vs 3.93 (21.1% fewer), Char Edits 76.46 vs 168.8 (54.7% fewer)
Cold-start ablation (Table VI, Task Failure): SFT+GRPO ASR = 16.7% vs GRPO-only ASR = 8.7% (Attack TER 80.0 vs 88.0); paper reports 5.8% relative ASR drop without cold-start

Tool usage by objective (Table IV): Task Failure avg 3.02 tool calls / 13.0 char edits (only ~10.6% of the 200-char budget); Action Inflation 3.16 calls / 122.1 edits; Constraint Violation 2.42 calls / 64.0 edits
Six victim VLAs each >90% clean task success; per-episode budget ≤200 char edits and ≤4 tool calls
baselines_compared: Frozen GPT-5-mini attacker using the same FIND→APPLY tool-calling interface (strong prompt-engineering baseline)
GRPO-only (no cold-start SFT) ablation of SABER itself
Prior VLA/robot attack methods cited as context (RoboPAIR, Wu et al. IROS'25, FreezeVLA, AttackVLA, VLA-Fool, BadVLA) — discussed, not re-run
